parents.mother_research_notes: Lydia (Griswold) Chaffee, b.Dec 17, 1722 Simsbury CT; d.Oct 1, 1801 Windsor CT. Daughter of the Griswold family of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Brother John was partner in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: Her older brother John Chaffee was a partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee — the business linking her birth family directly to her husband's. The 1784 marriage to Captain James Hooker thus joined both personal and commercial lines of the Windsor Connecticut Chaffee household.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived 41 years past husband's 1805 death
narrative_blocks.body: She survived her husband Captain James Hooker by forty-one years — he died December 1805, she lived until October 1846 at eighty-six. After James's death she returned from Rochester New York to Windsor Connecticut and lived with her unmarried sister Hepsibah Chaffee until Hepsibah's 1834 death.

parents.mother_research_notes: Lydia (Griswold) Chaffee, b.Dec 17, 1722 Simsbury CT; d.Oct 1, 1801 Windsor CT. Daughter of the Griswold family of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Brother John was partner in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: Her older brother John Chaffee was a partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee — the business linking her birth family directly to her husband's. The 1784 marriage to Captain James Hooker thus joined both personal and commercial lines of the Windsor Connecticut Chaffee household.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived 41 years past husband's 1805 death
narrative_blocks.body: She survived her husband Captain James Hooker by forty-one years — he died December 1805, she lived until October 1846 at eighty-six. After James's death she returned from Rochester New York to Windsor Connecticut and lived with her unmarried sister Hepsibah Chaffee until Hepsibah's 1834 death.

cross_connections.display_label: His mother — daughter of Governor Joseph Talcott.
His maternal grandfather, Governor of Connecticut 1724-1741.
First cousin once removed via the Talcott family.
Wife of his cousin Wadsworth — also a Hooker descendant.
Father-in-law and partner in the Hooker and Chaffee firm.
his partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee
brother of his wife Mary Chaffee

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker and Chaffee supplied the Continental Army
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker and Chaffee was one of New England's largest mercantile houses before the Revolution, transacting much business for the Colonial Government. The partners gave freely to the patriot cause and were seriously embarrassed at war's end.

narrative_blocks.header: His firm provisioned alongside Wadsworth's commissary
narrative_blocks.body: The Hooker and Chaffee firm operated alongside Jeremiah Wadsworth's larger commissary operation — both Hooker-Talcott descendants provisioning the same Continental Army. The Connecticut River Valley supply network was effectively a Hooker-tree internal collaboration.

narrative_blocks.header: His Windsor house is on the National Register
narrative_blocks.body: He built the James Hooker House ca. 1772 at 118 Palisado Avenue, Windsor — now part of the Palisado Avenue Historic District on the National Register of Historic Places. The interior was later gutted for Chaffee School classrooms but the Federal-period exterior survives under Greek Revival additions.

institutions.institution_blurb: Silent partner; one of New England's great Revolutionary-era trading houses.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He built this house c.1772; it survives on Palisado Avenue's National Register district.

bio.title: Captain
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Captain James Hooker (1742-1805), Windsor CT merchant.

career.notes: Principal of INST070 Hooker and Chaffee. Succeeded to father's Hartford business with brothers; established Windsor branch. See INST070 for full firm history.

cross_connections.display_label: His sisters' graves share her husband's burial plot at Vale

bio.bio_blurb: Hooker descendant; wife of Rev. Andrew Yates

burial.plot_notes: Vale Cemetery Schenectady NY per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Husband's ward became consort of Hawaii's last queen
narrative_blocks.body: The Dominis family — Captain John Dominis, wife Mary Jones, and their young son John Owen — boarded at 26 Front Street, the Yates home in Schenectady; John Owen was born there on March 10, 1832, and the Dominis daughters who died young were buried in the Yates family plot at Vale Cemetery alongside Hannah and her husband. That same boy went on to marry Queen Liliʻuokalani, the last sovereign monarch of the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi, becoming the kingdom's last and only male consort.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Union College founding professor and clergyman

burial.plot_notes: Vale Cemetery Schenectady NY per FA

education.notes: Per Yale bio reference: "the Rev. Dr. Andrew Yates (Yale 1794)."

career.notes: One of the first professors at Union College per Schenectady biographical records. "Well versed in ancient and modern languages including Holland Dutch."

cross_connections.display_label: Both connected to John Owen Dominis's Hawaiian royal orbit
Born in his Schenectady home on March 10, 1832

narrative_blocks.header: The Dominis family lived in his Schenectady home
narrative_blocks.body: The Schenectady Gazette (August 27, 1932) records that John Owen Dominis was 'born March 10, 1832 at 26 Front Street in the home of Reverend Dr. Andrew Yates' — the Dominis family boarded with Yates while the sea-captain father traded abroad, and the Dominis daughters who died at boarding school were buried in Christopher Yates's family plot at Vale Cemetery, where Yates and Hannah Allin Hooker are also interred. That boarder's son went on to marry Queen Liliʻuokalani, the last sovereign monarch of the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi, making a Schenectady parsonage the birthplace of the kingdom's final male consort.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson served Farragut's staff at Mobile Bay
narrative_blocks.body: Andrew Yates's grandson, Captain Arthur Reed Yates (Naval Academy 1857) — son of his eldest son Reverend John Austin Yates — served on Admiral David Farragut's personal staff at the Battle of Mobile Bay on August 5, 1864, receiving Farragut's written commendation for gallantry and dying in November 1892 on the very eve of promotion to Commodore. At that same battle, Captain James Hooker Strong — whose mother Dolly Goodwin Hooker was the own sister of Yates's wife Hannah Allin Hooker — commanded USS Monongahela and rammed the Confederate ironclad CSS Tennessee, making two men bound through this Schenectady household heroes of the same decisive naval engagement.

narrative_blocks.header: One of Union College's founding professors in Schenectady
narrative_blocks.body: Reverend Andrew Yates was among the first professors at Union College in Schenectady — an institution chartered in 1795 whose founders included his cousin Joseph C. Yates, later Governor of New York (1823-1825). His command of ancient and modern languages, including Holland Dutch, made him one of the most learned academics in upstate New York of his generation, and he later served as principal of the Polytechnic Institute in Chittenango, New York.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife was kin to Texas's founder
narrative_blocks.body: Andrew Yates's first wife Mary Austin (b.1776; d.1806) was described in a Schenectady biographical record as 'of English stock and a relative of the founder of Austin, Texas' — a reference to Stephen F. Austin (1793-1836), the American empresario whose colonization of Mexican Texas made 'Austin' a legendary name in the story of westward expansion. The precise kinship is not documented, but the community clearly recognized the connection, and Yates honored his first wife's memory by naming their daughter Mary Austin — a name that eventually passed to his granddaughter Mary Austin Yates Stuyvesant, buried at Saint Mark's-in-the-Bowery in New York City.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
FindAGrave memorial records

bio.bio_blurb: Hooker descendant who married into the Stuyvesants

burial.plot_notes: Saint Marks Church-in-the-Bowery Churchyard per FA

naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Mary Austin' after her father Rev. Andrew Yates's first wife Mary Austin (d.1806) — honoring his first family in the second.

narrative_blocks.header: Both Reade sisters married into the Hooker family
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Austin Yates married John Reade Stuyvesant in 1840 — whose father Nicholas William Stuyvesant had married Catherine Livingston Reade, the sister of Helen Sarah Reade who had married Mary's uncle James Hooker of Poughkeepsie (H00797). The Reade sisters thus each connected to the Hooker world: one by marrying James Hooker directly, the other through her son who married back into the Hooker line a generation later.

narrative_blocks.header: Named her adopted daughter Hannah for her grandmother
narrative_blocks.body: Ann Eliza Yates Ireland and her husband John R. Ireland adopted a child they named Hannah — the same first name as Ann's grandmother Hannah Allin Hooker, daughter of Captain James Hooker of Windsor. The name carried the Hooker-Yates heritage forward even into an adoptive relationship.

naming_inspiration.description: First name 'Thomas' likely honors Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford CT.
Middle name 'Hooker' continues the Hooker family name.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Thomas Hooker; died at eleven months old
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker Yates was named in full for Reverend Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford and the progenitor of the Hooker line — an extraordinary naming act in the Yates household six generations from that same Thomas Hooker. He died on August 5, 1827, eleven months after his birth, never knowing the weight of the name he carried.

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker women who married Strongs in 1729 and 1813

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband was Monroe County's first judge
narrative_blocks.body: Dolly Goodwin Hooker married Elisha Beebe Strong, who in 1821 became the first judge of Monroe County, New York. She died in Rochester in 1850; he remarried and moved to Detroit, dying there in 1867.

narrative_blocks.header: Her Hooker-Strong marriage echoed one from 1729
narrative_blocks.body: When Dolly Goodwin Hooker married Elisha Beebe Strong in 1813, she entered a family that had already wed into the Hooker line 84 years before: Captain Asahel Strong Jr. had married her distant cousin Sarah Hooker at Farmington in 1729, and a grandson of that marriage — Asahel Hooker Strong — was already carrying her maiden name as his own middle name by 1776. Dolly's marriage completed the second arc; her great-grandson Beebe Hooker Strong, born in Milwaukee in 1866, would carry the name to a third generation on the Wisconsin frontier.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester NY per FA
Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester NY per FA

notable.notable_blurb: First Judge of Monroe County, NY; Yale 1809

education.notes: Prepared for college by Nathan Johnson. Yale class of 1809.
Attended Litchfield Law School 1810; thereafter read law in Canandaigua NY.

career.notes: Partner with William H. Adams. Admitted to bar 1812.
Purchased 1,000 acres near Rochester with Elisha Beach; developed Carthage area; erected storehouses, incline railway, bridge over lower falls.
Elected from Ontario County.
Appointed March 5, 1821 by Governor DeWitt Clinton. Monroe County organized February 23, 1821.
Moved to Detroit 1861; died there Oct 14, 1867.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended 1810; appointed first judge of Monroe County NY by 1821.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Strong men who married Hooker descendants, 84 years apart

narrative_blocks.header: First Judge of Monroe County, appointed 1821
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Beebe Strong was appointed the first judge of Monroe County, New York on March 5, 1821 by Governor DeWitt Clinton — just days after the county itself was organized by the state legislature from Ontario County territory. A Yale graduate (1809) and Litchfield Law School alumnus, he had also served in the New York State Assembly in 1819-1820 and as a presidential elector in 1824, becoming one of the most prominent legal figures in upstate New York.

narrative_blocks.header: His Hooker marriage repeated a Strong family tradition
narrative_blocks.body: When Elisha Beebe Strong married Dolly Goodwin Hooker in 1813 at Canandaigua, New York, he was not the first Strong to wed a Thomas Hooker descendant: 84 years earlier, his ancestral kin Captain Asahel Strong Jr. had married Sarah Hooker of Farmington, Connecticut in 1729, producing a line that named a grandson 'Asahel Hooker Strong' to preserve the memory. The two unions — Farmington 1729, Canandaigua 1813 — trace the same Strong-Hooker affinity across the Great Migration and the early Republic, from a Farmington lawyer's household to a frontier judge's courtroom on the Erie Canal.

bio.photo_notes: Rear Admiral James Hooker Strong, USN (1814-1882)

burial.plot_notes: Green-Wood Cemetery Brooklyn per FA/Wikipedia

notable.notable_blurb: Rear Admiral; Battle of Mobile Bay hero, 1864

education.notes: Interrupted studies Feb 2, 1829 to accept naval appointment as midshipman.

career.notes: Full career: Midshipman Feb 2, 1829; Passed Midshipman Jun 4, 1836; Lieutenant Sep 1841; Commander Apr 24, 1861; Captain Aug 5, 1865; Commodore Mar 21, 1870; Rear Admiral Sep 1873; retired Apr 25, 1876 after 47 years service.

military_service.unit: USS Mohawk, USS Flag (South Atlantic Blockading Squadron); USS Monongahela (West Gulf Blockading Squadron)
military_service.notes: May 8, 1862: captured schooner Amelia off Charleston aboard USS Flag (blockade runner with cotton). Aug 5, 1864: Battle of Mobile Bay — commanding USS Monongahela, rammed Confederate ironclad CSS Tennessee to shield Farragut flagship USS Hartford; promoted to Captain for this action. Commanded USS Canandaigua in Mediterranean 1869-70. Commanded South Atlantic Squadron as Rear Admiral 1873-75. Retired Apr 25, 1876.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors the maternal Hooker lineage.

cross_connections.display_label: Both fought at Mobile Bay under Admiral Farragut, 1864

narrative_blocks.header: Rammed the Confederate ironclad to shield Admiral Farragut
narrative_blocks.body: Commanding USS Monongahela at the Battle of Mobile Bay on August 5, 1864, James Hooker Strong drove his ship into the Confederate ironclad CSS Tennessee to protect Admiral Farragut's flagship USS Hartford from a ramming attack — exposing Monongahela to point-blank broadsides that destroyed her iron cutwater. His initiative contributed to Tennessee's surrender after ninety minutes of combat, earned him promotion to captain, and stands as the defining action of the last great naval battle of the Civil War.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Navy destroyers later bore his name
narrative_blocks.body: Two United States Navy ships bore the name USS Strong in honor of Rear Admiral James Hooker Strong: DD-467, a Fletcher-class destroyer commissioned in 1943 and sunk by Japanese aircraft off Bougainville in the Solomon Islands in November 1943; and DD-758, later sold to Brazil in 1973 as the Rio Grande del Norte, which served through the Korean and Vietnam Wars before being sunk in a storm off Durban, South Africa in 1997 while under tow to the scrapyard. His middle name 'Hooker' was a deliberate genealogical honor — his mother Dolly Goodwin Hooker made him one of the most celebrated figures to carry the Hooker family name.

narrative_blocks.header: Enrolled at the school his uncle later led
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker Strong enrolled at the Polytechnic College in Chittenango, New York around 1827 before his 1829 naval appointment — the same institution where his aunt's husband, Reverend Andrew Yates (who had married Hannah Allin Hooker, sister of Strong's mother Dolly), would later serve as principal. The Hooker family's deep connections to the Schenectady–Chittenango educational corridor shaped Strong's early formation, and the Polytechnic College's technical emphasis in mathematics and applied science gave him the precise preparation the Navy sought in its post-War of 1812 officer cohort.

narrative_blocks.header: His aunt's husband's grandson fought the same battle
narrative_blocks.body: Captain Arthur Reed Yates — grandson of Reverend Andrew Yates, who had married Strong's aunt Hannah Allin Hooker — served on Admiral Farragut's personal staff at the very same Battle of Mobile Bay on August 5, 1864, earning Farragut's written commendation for gallantry; he died in 1892 on the very eve of promotion to Commodore. Two men bound by the same Schenectady household — one commanding a ship, the other on the admiral's staff — were both at the decisive moment of the last great naval engagement of the Civil War.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester NY per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Bank cashier at Batavia; dead at twenty-two
narrative_blocks.body: Julian Elisha Strong, named for his mother's family (the Elisha Beebe Strongs had a tradition of honoring elder kin), became a bank cashier at Batavia, New York — a modest career cut short when he died on September 15, 1838, at age twenty-two, unmarried. His name lived on: his sister Martha Cornelia named her son Julien Elisha Winder in his honor, a generational echo that survived him by 75 years.

narrative_blocks.body: Martha Cornelia Strong was born as a twin with her sister Bertha Rosabella on April 13, 1818 in Rochester, New York. Bertha died in 1846 at age twenty-eight; Martha survived as a starred entry in the Hooker genealogy.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Home Cemetery Milwaukee WI per FA

career.notes: Served during winter 1839-40.

career.organization: Kellogg & Strong Steamboat Company and Warehouse, Milwaukee
career.notes: Prominent Milwaukee merchant.

narrative_blocks.header: Helped secure the land for Forest Home Cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Hunter Strong was instrumental in securing the land for Forest Home Cemetery in Milwaukee and persuading St. Paul's Episcopal Church to establish the cemetery there — a civic contribution that anchored Milwaukee's most prominent burial ground. He came to Wisconsin around 1830, clerked in the territorial legislature in 1839-40, built his fortune through the Kellogg & Strong Steamboat company and Newberry Furnace, and was twice married.

narrative_blocks.header: Bridged two Strong-Hooker marriages and two frontier states
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Hunter Strong was the son of the second Strong-Hooker marriage — Judge Elisha Beebe Strong and Dolly Goodwin Hooker — and the father of the man who would carry that name to a third generation: Beebe Hooker Strong, born Milwaukee 1866. He came to Wisconsin around 1830 at age ten, making the journey from the Rochester land grants his father had built to the Great Lakes frontier, and with him the Strong-Hooker legacy crossed into a new American West.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
History of Descendants of Elder John Strong, Vol. 1 (1871)

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors the maternal Hooker lineage.

narrative_blocks.header: Died young; daughter bequeathed to sister in Detroit
narrative_blocks.body: Eliza Hooker Strong Mumford died on June 5, 1844, at age twenty-one — just twenty months after the birth of her daughter Eliza Mumford — and by the terms of her will, bequeathed the child to her sister Martha Cornelia Strong Winder of Detroit, who 'adopted her as her own' under the name Eliza Mumford Winder. The child grew up among the Winder children of Detroit, married Charles B. Northrup in 1867, and carried both the Mumford and Winder names as testament to her mother's brief life.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his dead half-brother Alexander Allin Hooker (b.1763; d.1781; FAG 22532503), son of Capt. James Hooker's second wife Hannah Allin — a memorial naming that also honors the Allin family name carried through Capt. James's second wife.

military_service.unit: Unknown — service documented in son's obituary
military_service.notes: Fought at Battle of Lundy's Lane (July 25, 1814) and Battle of Queenston Heights (Oct 13, 1812). Documented in obituary of his son Capt. James Henry Hooker, Rochester newspaper, March 1907: 'His father, Alexander B. Hooker, served in the war of 1812, fighting in the battles of Lundy's Lane, Queenstown, and others.'

burial.plot_notes: Hooker Cemetery — land he deeded to Irondequoit in 1840; family plot survived 1950s accidental destruction

narrative_blocks.header: Pioneer settler who gave Irondequoit its Hooker Cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: Alexander Allin Hooker arrived in the Rochester area in 1816 — with his brother Horace — to visit their sister Dolly (Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong, who had recently married and settled in Canandaigua) and stayed to open a general store that lasted three years; by 1828 he had purchased Jesse Tainter's farm and by 1839 he was Irondequoit's first Town Clerk, and in 1840 he deeded land that became Hooker Cemetery to the town. His family burial plot there is one of the few in the cemetery to survive intact when the town accidentally destroyed most of the grounds in the 1950s, leaving his stone among the more legible remnants of the family that gave Irondequoit its first civic institutions.

narrative_blocks.header: Fought at Lundy's Lane before founding Irondequoit
narrative_blocks.body: Before settling in the Rochester area, Alexander Allin Hooker served in the War of 1812, fighting at the battles of Lundy's Lane (July 25, 1814, one of the bloodiest engagements on the Canadian border) and Queenston Heights — service documented in his son's 1907 newspaper obituary, which called him 'a soldier' who 'served in the war of 1812, fighting in the battles of Lundy's Lane, Queenstown, and others.' He came to Rochester in 1816, the year after the war ended, and within two decades had deeded a cemetery and founded a town — a veteran who became a civic patriarch.

narrative_blocks.header: An old Indian trail passed behind his cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: The burying ground Alexander Hooker made public in 1840 sat on a sandy knoll traversed by an Old Indian Trail that ran from the Seneca Village on Boughton Hill (near Victor, New York) to the Indian Landing at the mouth of the Genesee River — meaning the Hooker Cemetery was not merely a settler burial ground but sat astride an ancient Seneca thoroughfare, and likely contains Seneca burials alongside those of the earliest European settlers. It is estimated that perhaps a thousand people are buried in the one-and-a-half acres, most without markers, interred by pioneers under pine and oak trees before the ground was formally enclosed.

burial.plot_notes: Hooker Cemetery Irondequoit — inscription: "She acted well her part and sleeps in Jesus"

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester — buried with husband Asahel Clark Kendrick

narrative_blocks.header: Died at fifty-three, survived by her professor husband
narrative_blocks.body: Helen MacNaughton Hooker Kendrick died on November 1, 1880 at age fifty-three — exactly fifteen years before her husband, Professor Asahel Clark Kendrick, died in the same Rochester home at age eighty-five after sixty years of teaching Greek at the university he had helped found in 1850. She and Kendrick were buried together at Mount Hope Cemetery, where he joined her in 1895, the dormitory that would bear his name not to be dedicated for another eighteen years.

narrative_blocks.header: Her marriage linked Hartford's Puritans to Rochester's founders
narrative_blocks.body: When Helen MacNaughton Hooker married Asahel Clark Kendrick in 1857, she connected the descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker of Hartford — the founding Puritan of Connecticut — to the man who had helped establish the University of Rochester seven years earlier and would teach Greek there for thirty-eight years. The union meant that two of Kendrick's children by Helen (Ryland and Josephine) were both Hooker descendants and heirs to a Rochester academic legacy, a thread that would run through four generations to Ann Kendrick McNabb in the twenty-first century.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
FindAGrave

burial.plot_notes: Hooker Cemetery Irondequoit — same cemetery his grandfather deeded to the town in 1840; buried alongside family

military_service.unit: Company E, 39th Regiment Illinois Volunteer Infantry (enlisted Aug 12, 1861; discharged May 26, 1862). Also cited as 189th Regt. Ill. Vol. Inf. Newspaper (March 1907) says he raised the "Fifty-ninth Illinois Infantry, known as the Florence Rifles" — conflicting unit numbers; all sources agree on rank of Captain.
military_service.notes: Enlisted August 12, 1861; discharged May 26, 1862. EH: 'saw much active service.' Will of 1904 left $200,000 for military school with President Roosevelt as executor; declared illegal.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousins: Civil War captain and WWII general
cross_connections.notes: H02027 Captain James Henry Hooker (Civil War; willed $200,000 for military school with Roosevelt as executor) and HD4700 GV Strong (WWII G-2 chief) represent the military tradition of the Hooker-Strong family across two American wars. H02027 is GV Strong's first cousin twice removed.

narrative_blocks.header: Left $200,000 for a military school; Roosevelt executor
narrative_blocks.body: In 1904, Captain James Henry Hooker executed a will leaving $200,000 to the United States government to found a naval and military school 'with high ethical standards,' naming President Theodore Roosevelt his executor — an act that drew national newspaper coverage when the will was revealed at his death, with the Rochester press reporting the $200,000 bequest under the headline 'Capt. Hooker's Will.' The will was subsequently declared illegal, the military school was never built, and Roosevelt never served as executor; but the ambition behind it — to endow a school 'with high ethical standards' — speaks to the character of a man who had watched his father fight at Lundy's Lane, served himself as a captain in the Illinois Volunteers, and never stopped thinking about military education.

narrative_blocks.header: Father fought in 1812; he led Illinois troops
narrative_blocks.body: Captain James Henry Hooker's father, Alexander Allin Hooker, had fought in the War of 1812 at the battles of Lundy's Lane and Queenston Heights — two of the bloodiest engagements on the Canadian border — and it was this family tradition of military service that James carried into the Civil War, enlisting as captain of Company E, 39th Regiment Illinois Volunteers in August 1861. Father and son are both buried in Hooker Cemetery in Irondequoit — the same ground Alexander Hooker himself had deeded to the town in 1840, a family burial plot that survived the 1950s accidental destruction that wiped most of the surrounding stones.

career.notes: Appointed Canal Commissioner of New York.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter's line became Gilded Age Manhattan royalty
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker's daughter Catherine Livingston Hooker married Colonel John William Hamersley in 1840 — a Manhattan lawyer, traveler, and social figure who had met Muhammad Ali Pasha in Egypt and helped save the Mexican Republic from Napoleon — and their line grew into one of Gilded Age New York's most prominent families: the Colonel's son J. Hooker Hamersley published poetry, once courted Edith Roosevelt before she chose Theodore, and whose son Louis Gordon Hamersley was born to international headlines as the 'seven million dollar baby' in 1892. The Hooker who had been a Windsor merchant's son and Poughkeepsie probate lawyer thus became, within three generations, the progenitor of a Fifth Avenue dynasty that hosted a President's Cup speedboat ceremony and twelve hundred crippled veterans on a Hudson River estate.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve years as Surrogate of Dutchess County
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker served as Surrogate of Dutchess County for twelve years from 1828 to 1840 — the probate judge responsible for wills, estates, and guardianships across one of New York's most prosperous Hudson Valley counties — and was selected as Presidential Elector of New York in 1836, before being appointed Canal Commissioner of New York in 1842, overseeing operations of the Erie Canal that had transformed the American interior. He had come to Poughkeepsie in 1813 after graduating Yale (1810) and studying at Litchfield Law School, the first law school in the United States, and spent his entire career in Dutchess County, dying there in September 1858.

narrative_blocks.header: Vestryman of Christ Church for forty-one years
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker served as Vestryman of Christ Church in Poughkeepsie from 1817 — the year after he married Helen Sarah Reade and settled in Dutchess County — until his death in 1858, forty-one consecutive years in the same Episcopal congregation through the entire arc of his career as Surrogate, Canal Commissioner, and retired counsellor. This was a quiet denominational shift: the Hookers were descendants of the Puritan Rev. Thomas Hooker, a Congregationalist founder of Hartford, yet James spent his adult life in the Episcopal church — a shift that would be echoed when his grandson J. Hooker Hamersley was eulogized at Grace Church on Fifth Avenue, the grandest Episcopal church in Manhattan.

narrative_blocks.header: He married into the Reade Street Livingstons
narrative_blocks.body: On January 24, 1816, James Hooker married Helen Sarah Reade in Poughkeepsie — the granddaughter of John Reade (1745-1808) and Catherine Livingston Reade, connecting the Windsor Hooker family to both the Livingston Manor dynasty and to the colonial New York merchant family whose ancestor Joseph Reade had served as warden of Trinity Church for fifty years, sat on the Governor's Provincial Council, and given Reade Street in Lower Manhattan its name. The marriage brought into the Hooker line a thread of colonial New York aristocracy that would eventually surface in his grandson J. Hooker Hamersley — a man who traced his ancestry to both Robert Livingston the Elder (grantee of Livingston Manor by royal charter) and the Reade family whose name still runs through Lower Manhattan.

burial.plot_notes: Poughkeepsie Rural Cemetery per FA

bio.photo_notes: Sarah Helen Reade Hooker (1790-1879); wife of Judge James Hooker

burial.plot_notes: Poughkeepsie Rural Cemetery per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Her family's name appears on a Manhattan street
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Helen Reade was the granddaughter of John Reade (1745-1808) and Catherine Livingston Reade — a member of the great Livingston family of the Hudson Valley — and her great-grandfather was Joseph Reade (1694-1771), the New York merchant and provincial councillor whose family gave Reade Street in Lower Manhattan its name, serving as warden of Trinity Church for over fifty years and counting among his in-laws the Mayor of New York City (1702-1703) and Frederick Philipse, one of the wealthiest Dutch merchants in colonial New York. She was also, through her Livingston grandmother, a fifth-generation descendant of Robert Livingston the Elder, the Scottish immigrant granted Livingston Manor by royal charter — a lineage her grandson James Hooker Hamersley would proudly enumerate in his biography.

narrative_blocks.header: Her great-grandfather advised delaying the Stamp Act
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Reade, Sarah's great-grandfather, sat on the New York Governor's Provincial Council from 1761 until his death in 1771, and — while serving under Governor Sir Henry Moore — advised the administration to delay the issuance of stamped paper required by the Stamp Act, positioning himself among the colonial establishment figures who helped defuse that crisis before revolution became inevitable. He also sat on the grand jury of the New York Slave Conspiracy trials of 1741, which based on questionable testimony resulted in death sentences for thirty-four defendants and the deportation of ninety-one others — a reminder that his distinguished family history also carried moral complexity at its center.

sources.label: FindAGrave
Americana Aristocracy — Helen Sarah Reade Hooker
Wikipedia — Joseph Reade (politician)

burial.plot_notes: Poughkeepsie Rural Cemetery per FA

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Livingston' honors her maternal grandmother Catherine Livingston Reade and the Livingston Manor family.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of a judge
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine Livingston Hooker was born in Poughkeepsie in 1817, the daughter of Judge James Hooker (Yale 1810, Erie Canal Commissioner) and Sarah Helen Reade, whose family gave Reade Street its name and descended from Robert Livingston the Elder — and she married Colonel John William Hamersley, a New York City lawyer and traveler who had met Muhammad Ali Pasha in Egypt, helped save the Mexican Republic from Napoleon, and whose Friday receptions at 255 Fifth Avenue drew the eminent writers and artists of the city. She was described by his obituary as 'a lady of rare abilities and sterling worth' — a Poughkeepsie judge's daughter who became a Manhattan society figure — and died in February 1867 at age forty-nine, leaving four children who would carry both the Hooker name and the Reade-Livingston ancestry into the Gilded Age.

sources.label: FindAGrave
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Died at eighteen
narrative_blocks.body: Helen Mary Hooker was born in Poughkeepsie on June 12, 1819 and died on January 24, 1838 at age eighteen — before her father's career as Canal Commissioner, before her older sister Catherine married Colonel Hamersley, and before the entire arc of the Gilded Age that her sister's family would inhabit. She left no descendants, while her sister Catherine's line extended through the J. Hooker Hamersley family into the 1970s, the Hooker name surviving through the Hamersley branch's middle names long after Helen Mary's early death in Poughkeepsie.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

parents.father_research_notes: James Hooker of Windsor CT — third wife Mary (Chaffee) Hooker.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary (Chaffee) Hooker (third wife of James Hooker)

notable.notable_blurb: Pioneer merchant; grandfather of Elon Hooker

careers.location: Bristol near Canandaigua NY → Carthage near Rochester NY
careers.notes: Per EH 798: 'He had the spirit of the pioneer and the glowing accounts of the Genesee Country attracted him to Western New York.' First lived briefly at Bristol near Canandaigua NY in mercantile business with brother Alexander Allin Hooker. Later moved to Carthage near Rochester to cooperate with brother-in-law Judge Strong (likely Elisha Beebe Strong 1788-1867, husband of Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong) in developing 'what was supposed to be the future city of Genesee.' For 20 years engaged in active commercial enterprises.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded Rochester Commercial Nursery in 1844
narrative_blocks.body: He co-founded the Rochester Commercial Nursery in 1844, part of the wave of New England transplants who built Rochester into the nursery and seed capital of America. His son Horace B. studied under Colonel Bissell, then joined the nursery before becoming an engineer and contractor.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson Elon founded Hooker Chemical Company
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson Elon Huntington Hooker founded the Hooker Electrochemical Company at Niagara Falls in 1909, pioneering American production of chlorine and caustic soda. Three generations of Rochester Hookers carried the entrepreneurial spirit Edward Hooker described as 'the spirit of the pioneer.'

career.organization: Mercantile partnership with brother Alexander Allin Hooker, Bristol NY
career.notes: First settled at Bristol near Canandaigua NY as a merchant after migrating from Windsor CT in 1821.

career.organization: Carthage NY (with brother-in-law Judge Elisha Beebe Strong)
career.notes: Moved to Carthage near Rochester to cooperate with brother-in-law in developing 'what was supposed to be the future city of Genesee.' Engaged in active commercial enterprises for about twenty years.
Co-founded with partners in 1844 — part of Rochester's emergence as the 'Flower City.' Confirmed by University of Rochester Special Collections (Hooker Family Papers, D.130, 1790-1963, 27 boxes).

narrative_blocks.header: Engaged in literary pursuits; died unmarried at seventy-nine
narrative_blocks.body: Frances Hooker never married and spent her life engaged in literary pursuits, dying on April 20, 1906 at Skaneateles, New York at age seventy-nine. She was the only child of Horace Hooker among nine to live to old age unmarried.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Chaffee' honors grandmother Mary Chaffee Hooker and the Chaffee family (X00433 Hezekiah Chaffee Jr.).

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.extra_names: EH 2039 records middle initial only ('Horace B.'); full middle name not specified.

military_service.notes: Per EH 2039: 'He was a lieutenant (acting captain) in the First Missouri Engineers during the Civil War.' The 1st Missouri Engineers was a Union Army engineering regiment. As a Hooker engineering forebear, his service prefigured the engineering careers of his sons (Elon, Harry, Paul, Horace W.).

sources.short: Wikipedia: Elon Huntington Hooker (Horace B. as father)

notable.notable_blurb: Civil War engineer; father of Elon Hooker
notable.primary_url_label: University of Rochester — Civil War Correspondence

narrative_blocks.header: Joined his engineer mentor in the Civil War
narrative_blocks.body: He had studied engineering under Colonel Josiah Bissell in Rochester; when war came, Bissell recruited him into his Engineer Regiment of the West. His companies repaired railroads and bridges throughout the western theater, including operations at Island No. Ten on the Mississippi.

narrative_blocks.header: Father's nursery led to H.B. Hooker and Son
narrative_blocks.body: After the war he returned to Rochester, joining his father's nursery before founding H.B. Hooker & Son contracting firm with his son Harry — one of Rochester's prominent construction companies. His son Elon Huntington Hooker would take the family's engineering tradition to Niagara Falls in 1909.

narrative_blocks.header: Wartime love letters scribbled over in blue
narrative_blocks.body: Hundreds of letters from Horace's Civil War service survive in the Rochester collection — and throughout them, every 'darling' and 'I love you' he wrote to Susan has been deliberately scribbled over in blue crayon. Whether Horace himself made the marks before sending, Susan censored them on receipt, or a later descendant edited the intimate lines, the question remains unanswered.

documents.blurb: Hundreds of his Civil War letters survive in this Rochester collection.

sources.short: Chaffee genealogy book, entry 702 (via mother Mary Chaffee)

naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Mary Chaffee' directly for her mother Mary (Chaffee) Hooker (X00319), who was herself named for the Chaffee family.

narrative_blocks.header: Named directly for her mother Mary Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Chaffee Hooker was named in full for her mother Mary Chaffee Hooker (X00319), making her name a direct echo of the generation before her. She married Dr. James Magoffin of Schenectady and died there on March 20, 1839 at age forty-two, nearly a decade before her husband.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-two; six children survived her
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Chaffee Hooker Magoffin died on March 27, 1839 in Schenectady at age forty-two, leaving behind Doctor James C. Magoffin and six children, three of whom carried her maiden name 'Hooker' as their middle name — a deliberate act of genealogical memory that would persist through four more generations and eventually surface in the name of the 55th Secretary of the Treasury. She traveled from Windsor, Connecticut to Schenectady and died before seeing any grandchild; but the 'Hooker' she left in her children's names traveled farther than she did.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery Schenectady per FA
First Presbyterian Church Cemetery Schenectady per FA. Inscription: "Elder in the Presbyterian Church who died in his 60th year." Surname listed as "McGaffin" in 1930s rubbings.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived his Hooker wife by eleven years
narrative_blocks.body: Doctor James C. Magoffin was a physician and elder of the Presbyterian Church in Schenectady — a man of both medicine and faith in a city becoming one of the first industrial centers of New York State — and outlived his wife Mary Chaffee Hooker Magoffin by eleven years, raising their six children after her death in 1839, three of whom bore her maiden name in their own names. His gravestone records him simply as 'Elder in the Presbyterian Church who died in his 60th year,' the epitaph of a man whose children's naming choices would echo through five generations.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
FindAGrave
FamilySearch

bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Reverend James Hooker Magoffin (1820-1883), Episcopal priest

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors his mother Mary Chaffee Hooker's maiden name — daughter of Captain James Hooker of Windsor, CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker to honor his mother's maiden name
narrative_blocks.body: Reverend James Hooker Magoffin carried 'Hooker' as his middle name to honor his mother Mary Chaffee Hooker, and passed the tradition forward — his son Frank was named 'Frank Hooker Magoffin,' making three consecutive generations of the Magoffin family to carry 'Hooker' as a given name. His grandson George Magoffin Humphrey became the 55th Secretary of the Treasury under President Eisenhower, carrying the heritage in the form of the middle name 'Magoffin.'

narrative_blocks.header: An Episcopal priest in a Presbyterian father's household
narrative_blocks.body: Reverend James Hooker Magoffin was ordained as an Episcopal priest despite being raised in a Presbyterian household — his father had been an elder in the Presbyterian Church in Schenectady — making him one of the quiet denominational crossovers of mid-nineteenth-century American religion, a son who took his faith in a different direction while preserving his mother's Hooker name in every way he could. He moved west from New York through Wisconsin, where his son Frank Hooker Magoffin was born in Waukesha in 1859, to Cheboygan County, Michigan, dying there in 1883 at sixty-three — following the American frontier even as an Episcopal clergyman.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Hill Cemetery Ann Arbor per FA

career.notes: Born in the town of Benton per FA. Served in Michigan area.

burial.plot_notes: Oakwood Cemetery, Syracuse, Onondaga County, New York.

bio.photo_notes: Henry Thomas Hooker (1803-1893), Rochester merchant and Syracuse salt manufacturer

career.notes: One of the early merchants of Rochester NY.
Contributed to Syracuse's salt industry through ingenuity and practical mechanical skill. Syracuse was known as the 'Salt City' of nineteenth-century America.

narrative_blocks.header: Early merchant of Rochester; salt manufacturer in Syracuse
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Thomas Hooker was one of the early merchants of Rochester, New York, before moving to Syracuse where he became a salt manufacturer — contributing, per his obituary, 'not a little in its earlier days' through 'his ingenuity and practical mechanical skill' to a salt industry that made Syracuse the Salt City of nineteenth-century America, one of the nation's most important inland commercial centers. A 'thorough gentleman of the old school,' athlete in his youth, and man of 'strictest integrity, honorable and honored in his dealings,' he was a member of the Presbyterian church whose 'habits of strict temperance' aided 'a vigorous and hearty old age' — so that at ninety, 'his erect form and firm step gave him the appearance of a much younger man.'

narrative_blocks.header: His ingenuity helped build the Syracuse salt industry
narrative_blocks.body: The salt industry that made Syracuse the 'Salt City' of nineteenth-century America benefited specifically from Henry Thomas Hooker's ingenuity and practical mechanical skill — his obituary credits him with contributions to salt manufacturing 'not a little in its earlier days,' when the Onondaga salt springs were being developed into an industrial complex producing much of the nation's table salt and helping finance the Erie Canal at a moment when inland transportation was remaking American commerce. He was also, by all accounts, a man who 'never wearied in his reminiscences of his early sports and adventures' — a former athlete who attributed his longevity to strict temperance habits, the obituary noting that even at the end of his life 'his erect form and firm step gave him the appearance of a much younger man,' as if the athlete were still in there somewhere.

burial.plot_notes: Oakwood Cemetery Syracuse per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her husband by four years at ninety-four
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Brown Cobb was born in Stonington, Connecticut in March 1803 — the same year as her husband Henry Thomas Hooker — and outlived him by four years, dying on December 29, 1897 at ninety-four; her obituary called her 'a woman of strong but lovely character — a true New England woman,' a charter member of the Fourth Presbyterian Church of Syracuse and later of the Memorial Presbyterian Church where her son Henry Cobb Hooker served as superintendent and elder. Born in the second year of Jefferson's presidency, she died in the year of McKinley's first inauguration, her ninety-four-year life spanning virtually the entire American republic as it grew from a coastal nation to a continental one.

burial.plot_notes: Oakwood Cemetery Syracuse per FA

career.notes: Connected with Spencer Clothing Company for approximately 30 years until his death.

career.role: Sunday school superintendent; Elder and Trustee
career.notes: Superintendent from 1887 until year before death; also elder and trustee. Originally superintendent of Scattergood Mission Sunday School until it was organized into Memorial Presbyterian Church in 1887.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Cobb' honors his mother Mary Brown Cobb Hooker (X01623).

narrative_blocks.header: Thirty years with Spencer Clothing; Sunday school superintendent
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Cobb Hooker spent approximately thirty years connected with the Spencer Clothing Company of Syracuse, and simultaneously devoted himself to the Memorial Presbyterian Church — serving as Sunday school superintendent from 1887 until failing health forced him out a year before his death, having shepherded the Sunday school from its origins as the Scattergood Mission through its organization into a full congregation, while also serving as elder and trustee. He died of sarcoma at midnight on May 25, 1908 at his summer cottage at Three Mile Point on Skaneateles Lake — a widower since the death of his wife Frances in 1895, survived only by his brother Thomas — his funeral held from his home at 108 West Castle Street in Syracuse.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Oakwood Cemetery Syracuse per FA

career.organization: Elbridge, New York and Lake Forest, Illinois
career.notes: Teacher until 1878.
EH: "since 1878 he has been engaged in manufacturing business at Syracuse, N.Y."

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at thirty-three; never remarried; lived to 1917
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker married Emma Cornelia 'Nellie' Dennison on September 26, 1872 in Syracuse, and she died less than three years later — on June 20, 1875, at age twenty-seven in East Syracuse, barely twenty-eight months into a marriage that left no children. Thomas lived forty-two more years as a widower, dying in Syracuse on June 21, 1917 at seventy-five — the last of the four children of Henry Thomas and Mary Brown Cobb Hooker to die, outlasting even his brother Henry Cobb by nine years.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

naming_inspiration.description: Named for Rev. Andrew Yates (I00899; Yale 1794), who married his father's cousin Hannah Allin Hooker (H00794). The 'Andrew Yates' name honored this prominent minister in the extended family.

cross_connections.display_label: Business partner in the Hooker and Chaffee mercantile firm.

narrative_blocks.header: Documented his enslaved Betty Stevenson by name
narrative_blocks.body: His 1791 statement to the Hartford town clerk recorded his ownership of 'a Negro Woman' and her newborn daughter 'called by the Name Betty Stevenson.' He registered the birth as legally required.

narrative_blocks.header: Held a high rank in Connecticut medicine
narrative_blocks.body: He came to Hartford to start a drug store and settled in Windsor in 1755 on the advice of Dr. Isaac Bull. He practiced there for sixty years until his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Born seven weeks after his father's death
narrative_blocks.body: His father Hezekiah Chaffee Sr. died in October 1731 and Hezekiah was born December 6 of the same year in Rehoboth, Massachusetts. He was the youngest of nine children.
